stories.title: Chicago/Midwest People & Company News, week of June 19, 2026
stories.excerpt: JLL announced the formation of the retail solutions group, a newly integrated team within JLL’s project and development services business to bring together design, architecture and project management capabilities throughout the entire brand lifecycle. Additionally, JLL tapped industry leader Mark Bateman as executive managing director to lead the strategic initiative and hired Kevin Songer as managing director of architecture for the group. Urban Innovations announced its property at 311 W Huron, a 15-story, 153,000-square-foot newly developed office building in Chicago’s River North neighborhood, has achieved Two Green Globes certification for new construction through the Green Building Initiative. Developed by Urban Innovations, designed by NORR, and constructed by ARCO/Murray, the certification recognizes the building’s sustainability and energy efficiency. The Chicago Housing Authority (CHA) recently hosted its 4th Annual Homeownership Forum at the David Rubenstein Forum at the University of Chicago, drawing more than 200 attendees for a day centered on homeownership. The event brought together current and prospective CHA homeowners who attended presentations and panel discussions. Through its public housing and voucher programs, CHA serves 135,000 people in 65,000 households across all 77 of Chicago’s community areas. Stream Realty Partners has been selected by Menashe Properties to provide property management services at 230 W. Monroe, a 29-story office tower totaling 625,000 square feet in Chicago’s West Loop. The assignment builds on Stream’s existing relationship with the property, which began three years ago when the firm was awarded the leasing assignment. Leader of Property Management & Partner Victoria Knudson will lead property management services, while Stream Managing Director Mark Bâby and Executive Vice President Ben Cleveland will continue to oversee leasing efforts on behalf of ownership. Lee & Associates negotiated a 42,169-square-foot l
bullets: JLL forms retail solutions group; hires Mark Bateman as executive managing director.
Urban Innovations' 153K SF office building at 311 W Huron achieves Two Green Globes.
Chicago Housing Authority serves 135K people across 77 community areas via public housing.
stories.summary: JLL reorganized its retail/design capabilities under new leadership. Urban Innovations completed a certified-green 153K SF office tower in River North. CHA operates 65K households citywide. Stream Realty expanded property management at 625K SF West Loop office tower for Menashe Properties.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. Reorganization at JLL and office-focused certifications do not signal material demand or supply shifts for rental apartments in Chicago. CHA homeownership forum is for-sale housing, not rental.

stories.title: JLL Arranges Debt, Equity for Luxury Condo Development in Downtown Lafayette
stories.excerpt: JLL Capital Markets has arranged $60.5 million in construction financing and additional joint venture equity for the development of Project Oak Hill, an 85-residence luxury condominium in downtown Lafayette. A JLL Capital Markets Debt and Equity Advisory team led by Matt Cimino and Jordan Angel arranged the construction loan with Centennial Bank and secured PCCP as the joint venture equity partner on behalf of Diamond Construction. Scheduled for completion in summer 2028, Oak Hill will deliver 23 one-bedroom, 41 two-bedroom and 21 three-bedroom residences, including 11 below-market-rate units, along with 2,000 square feet of ground-floor retail. The project will feature an amenity collection that includes a golf simulator, a private conference room, an indoor and outdoor fitness center, communal landscaped outdoor living rooms and workspaces, a Japanese-inspired Zen garden, fire pits, a private dog run, a bike-share program, and a private, elevated outdoor lounge. The Lafayette BART station sits only steps from the front door, putting residents aboard a direct train that reaches Embarcadero, San Francisco’s first stop across the bay, in roughly 30 minutes. The post JLL Arranges Debt, Equity for Luxury Condo Development in Downtown Lafayette appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JLL arranges $60.5M construction + equity for 85-unit luxury condo in downtown Lafayette.
Oak Hill delivers summer 2028; includes 11 below-market-rate units and 2K SF retail.
Lafayette BART station steps away; 30-min direct rail to SF Embarcadero.
stories.summary: Project Oak Hill, an 85-unit luxury condo tower in downtown Lafayette near BART, closed $60.5M in construction financing (Centennial Bank) and equity co-invest (PCCP) via JLL. Mixed 1-, 2-, 3-bed units; 11 deed-restricted affordable. Completion summer 2028. Transit proximity and luxury amenities (golf sim, Zen garden, dog run) target high-income buyers.
stories.impact: For-sale condo, not rental multifamily. However, the density and transit-oriented positioning in the East Bay signals strong demand near BART for mixed-income housing; Lafayette's emerging as a satellite growth node may push rental demand up the line in less-saturated East Bay submarkets as SF Bay Area rents remain elevated.

stories.title: Vilson Lumaj Plans Two 99-Unit Buildings in the Bronx After $14M Deal
stories.excerpt: Housing developer Vilson Lumaj has purchased a vacant property in the Bronx to build two 99-unit residential buildings, Commercial Observer has learned. The property at 761-769 East Tremont Avenue was sold for $10 million by real estate developer Yoram Eliyahu. The buyer also acquired 60,000 square feet of air rights above the property for $3.5 million from Michael and Rudolf Abramov, according to broker Rosewood Realty Group. The total sale price was $13.5 million. The development plans for two 99-unit buildings were meant to cut down on labor costs while still getting more units on the market, according to Rosewood’s Jonah Corney. “The development is going to be two 99-unit buildings which will be a work-around for the prevailing wage required under 485-x,” Corney told CO. “I guess it’s a sign of poor policy.” Corney represented the buyer alongside Rosewood’s Mike Kerwin and Mark Steinmetz. The proliferation of 99-unit developments throughout New York City has resulted in criticism of the state government for allegedly creating a piece of flawed policy after the expiration of the 421-a property tax abatement, as under its successor, the 485-x tax program, construction costs can increase 20 to 30 percent, depending on who you ask. Critics, such as the Real Estate Board of New York, have said that the tax program prevents the city from reaching its goals of meeting the needs of the national housing shortage. But it appears developers are simply building 99 units across multiple buildings, such as with Ami Weinstock working on six 99-unit buildings in Jamaica, Queens, in 2025, along with a scattering of other projects. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Vilson Lumaj buys Bronx site for $13.5M ($10M land + $3.5M air rights).
Planned: two 99-unit buildings (198 units total) to avoid prevailing wage under 485-x.
Address: 761-769 E Tremont Ave, Mott Haven; seller Yoram Eliyahu; air rights from Abramo family.
stories.summary: Developer Vilson Lumaj acquired three Bronx parcels in Mott Haven for $13.5M total to build two separate 99-unit residential buildings, circumventing New York State's prevailing wage mandate under the 485-x incentive program. Broker cited labor cost savings as rationale. The 99-unit subdivision is a common arbitrage structure proliferating across NYC.
stories.impact: This is a clear signal of supply-side distortion in the Bronx and NYC broadly: state wage policy is fragmenting project economics and forcing developers into two-building solutions to hit feasibility. The 198 units planned here likely carry higher per-unit costs to cover wage gaps vs. market rents, compressing developer returns and potentially delaying starts or reducing quality. Watch for downstream pressure on Mott Haven rents if affordability and operations quality suffer under this wage structure.

stories.title: New York People and Company News, Week of June 19, 2026
stories.excerpt: Greystone announced that CRE finance veteran Nicholas M. Gesue has been named COO. With more than 25 years of experience building and scaling national CRE finance platforms, Gesue most recently served as CEO of NewPoint Real Estate Capital Arqline has appointed Dixie L. Anderson as EVP of Technology Solutions & Infrastructure, further strengthening the leadership team as the company continues to scale its integrated operating platform across multifamily and build-to-rent housing. Most recently, Anderson served as SVPt at RPM Living, where she oversaw enterprise systems, product strategy, and technology initiatives supporting one of the nation’s largest multifamily portfolios. Odyssey Retail Advisors’ has appointed Josh Lewin as VP.With more than 20 years of retail real estate experience, Lewin has advised leading fashion, luxury, and lifestyle brands on their North American expansion strategies. He previously spent 17 years at Isaacs and Company Commercial Real Estate and most recently served as VP at Alvarez & Marsal Property Solutions Suffolk, a national construction management firm headquartered in Boston, has hired Melissa Kiefer as VP of Strategy, strengthening the company’s healthcare and life sciences efforts across New York, New Jersey and Connecticut. She brings more than 15 years of experience guiding regulatory strategy, capital execution and organizational alignment across complex institutional investments. Kiefer joins Suffolk from Hospital for Special Surgery. Carson Living, a leading provider of 24/7 Live Remote Doorman service, integrated access control, and resident engagement software for multifamily properties, has appointed Josh Evans as CEO. Evans succeeds founder Guy Blachman, who will continue to serve Carson as an advisor to the board. CRH, a leading provider of building materials, has elected Tony Will, effective to its board of directors, effective July 1. Will served as president, CEO and board member of CF Industries H
bullets: Greystone hires Nicholas M. Gesue as COO; 25+ yrs CRE finance, ex-NewPoint CEO.
Arqline appoints Dixie L. Anderson EVP Technology; ex-RPM Living enterprise systems lead.
Carson Living appoints Josh Evans as CEO; company provides 24/7 remote doorman, access control for multifamily.
stories.summary: Three leadership hires: Greystone adds financing veteran Gesue as COO; Arqline strengthens tech/ops via Anderson (ex-RPM); Carson Living installs Evans as CEO for resident engagement tech. Also noted: Odyssey Retail hires Josh Lewin (retail), Suffolk hires Melissa Kiefer (healthcare/life sciences construction strategy).
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact. Executive moves reflect capital market confidence and tech adoption within platforms, but do not signal material changes to supply, demand, or cost structures. However, Arqline's focus on integrated multifamily operating platforms and Carson's resident tech expansion suggest the industry is consolidating on software/ops efficiency—favorable for scale developers but a cost headwind for smaller operators.

stories.title: Menashe Properties Expands Chicago Office Portfolio
stories.excerpt: Oregon-based Menashe Properties has once again expanded its Chicago office portfolio with the $56.5 million acquisition of 180 N. LaSalle, a 38-story, 800,000-square-foot landmark tower in the heart of the city’s Central Loop. Menashe first entered the Chicago market in 2023 with the acquisition of 230 West Monroe, a 29-story, 707,000-square-foot office tower. In 2025, the company bought 125 S. Wacker Drive in the city’s West Loop.180 N. LaSalle offers immediate access to Chicago’s financial district, public transit, and the James R. Thompson Center redevelopment, which is expected to bring more than 2,000 Google employees in the coming year. “Chicago presents some of the most compelling office investment opportunities in the country,” said Jordan Menashe, CEO of Menashe. Menashe has retained Stream Realty Partners to oversee business operations, with Managing Director Mark Bâby and Executive Vice President Wendy Katz managing leasing for the property. Director Brian Keaty will lead property management and tenant services. The post Menashe Properties Expands Chicago Office Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Menashe Properties acquires 180 N. LaSalle (800K SF, 38-story) for $56.5M in Central Loop.
Menashe's third Chicago office buy since 2023; also owns 230 W Monroe, 125 S. Wacker.
Proximity to Google's 2K-person James R. Thompson Center move-in; Stream Realty retained for ops.
stories.summary: Oregon-based Menashe Properties expanded its Chicago office footprint to ~2.3M SF across three assets with the $56.5M acquisition of 180 N. LaSalle, a landmark Central Loop tower. Deal capitalized on proximity to Google's imminent Thompson Center relocation (2K+ employees), a major employment anchor in downtown. Stream Realty manages both this asset and Menashe's 230 W Monroe tower.
stories.impact: Signals investor confidence in Chicago office stabilization tied to Google's 2K-job relocation. While Menashe is office-focused, Google's in-migration and job creation in the Loop will lift regional employment and worker population, driving demand for apartment communities near downtown transit corridors and Loop adjacencies. Developers should monitor Google's Thompson Center ramp timeline for lease-up and the resulting commuting patterns to de-risk multifamily site selection and transit-oriented positioning in River North, West Loop, and South Loop submarkets.

stories.title: Related, Battery Park City Authority to Expand Affordable Housing at Tribeca Park
stories.excerpt: The Knicks parade wasn’t the only noise happening in Manhattan’s Battery Park on Thursday. Related Companies and the Battery Park City Authority (BPCA) have teamed up to preserve and expand the number of affordable units at Tribeca Park, Related’s 27-story Lower Manhattan residential building, the partners announced Thursday. Related built the 369-unit rental tower at 400 Chambers Street near Battery Park in 1999. The new agreement with BPCA amends the building’s ground lease to keep in place the 81 units already designated as affordable housing for individuals earning 50 percent of the area medium income. The agreement also will add 20 more units for people earning up to 130 percent of the area medium income. “We’re proud to work with Related to preserve and increase the number of affordable units in Tribeca Park,” Raju Mann, president and CEO of BPCA, said in a statement. “BPCA is committed to using any and every opportunity we have to add affordable homes to this neighborhood.” As part of the agreement, 101 units, or 25 percent of the building’s total residences, will be income-restricted through the remainder of the building’s lease term in 2069. Previously, the affordability at 400 Chambers Street was going to expire at the end of 2029. The agreement also increases the building’s ground rent payments to BPCA. “Battery Park City stands as one of New York City’s most successful mixed-income communities, and Related has been honored to be part of its evolution for more than four decades,” Bruce A. Beal Jr., president of Related Companies, said in a statement. “Maintaining and expanding affordable housing is critical to the city’s long-term economic strength and quality of life.” Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Related Companies & Battery Park City Authority expand affordable units at Tribeca Park (400 Chambers St).
Keep 81 existing affordable units at 50% AMI; add 20 units at 130% AMI.
Building: 369-unit rental tower completed 1999; ground lease amendment preserves affordability.
stories.summary: Related and the Battery Park City Authority amended the ground lease for Tribeca Park (369-unit rental building completed in 1999) to preserve 81 existing affordable units (50% AMI) and add 20 new affordable units (130% AMI). The initiative demonstrates BPCA's commitment to affordability preservation in Lower Manhattan and caps 27-story tower at 400 Chambers Street.
stories.impact: For-sale/mixed-income rental, not core market-rate multifamily development. However, the expansion of 130% AMI units (workforce housing) in Lower Manhattan signals regulatory and investor appetite for mixed-income rentals near waterfront/Battery Park; BPCA's active involvement in affordability expansion may encourage similar negotiations on future ground leases and create political leverage for developers seeking zoning or incentive concessions in exchange for deed-restricted affordable units.

stories.title: Post Brothers President Matthew Pestronk On D.C.’s $750M Office Conversion
stories.excerpt: One of the markets that has struggled the most with office real estate’s long post-pandemic correction happens to be home to one of the nation’s biggest conversion projects. Construction is underway on the Geneva, a $750 million project to turn a Class C office building in Washington, D.C., into a 532-unit multifamily complex with 61 affordable apartments and 57,000 square feet of retail. It’s the largest office-to-residential conversion ever in D.C., and bigger than any current project of its kind outside of New York. Post Brothers, the Philadelphia-based development firm running the show, recently secured a $575 million financing package, led by a record $465 million Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy (C-PACE) loan from Nuveen Green Capital, and a $110 million senior loan from Mavik Capital. Residents will start moving in next year. Commercial Observer spoke with Matthew Pestronk, co-founder and president of Post Brothers, about how the Geneva penciled, how the financing came together, and what cities get right and wrong about conversions. This conversation has been edited for length and clarity.   The Geneva will be a 532-unit multifamily complex with 61 affordable apartments and 57,000 square feet of retail. RENDERING: Courtesy Post Brothers Commercial Observer: Can you tell me about the Geneva, which is being described as the biggest office-to-resi project in D.C. history? Matthew Pestronk: It’s a lot more than that. It’s going to be the single most valuable piece of real estate in Washington, D.C., probably. It’s the best location anyone’s been able to build a rental apartment building anywhere in the United States, maybe in my career. It was previously encumbered by a Class C office building with a lot of long-term leases, and the neighborhood surrounding it was $5 million to $20 million houses, and there’s very rarely any residential properties available for large-scale development in a neighborhood like that. Can yo
bullets: Post Brothers' Geneva: $750M office-to-resi conversion, 532 units + 61 affordable, 57K SF retail.
Largest office-to-resi conversion in D.C.; financing: $465M C-PACE (Nuveen), $110M senior (Mavik).
D.C. market struggling with office; conversion pencils due to strong rental demand and transit access.
stories.summary: Post Brothers is executing the Geneva, a $750M office-to-residential conversion of a Class C office tower into 532 rental units (61 affordable) plus retail in Washington, D.C. The $575M financing package—led by a record $465M C-PACE loan from Nuveen Green Capital—demonstrates creative debt structures enabling conversion economics in a soft office market. Delivery timeline and lease-up strategy tied to D.C.'s post-pandemic demand recovery.
stories.impact: The Geneva exemplifies how weak office fundamentals in secondary gateway cities (D.C., outside NYC) create opportunistic conversion platforms for multifamily developers with patient capital and strong operations teams. The record C-PACE financing signals lender appetite for climate-backed debt on conversions; developers should monitor PACE availability in their target markets as a lever to underwrite older office/industrial stock for resi conversion. However, D.C.'s conversion success depends on sustained rental demand; watch absorption and rent growth post-opening to gauge whether broader metro job/population trends support 532-unit leap.

stories.title: EOS Adds to New England Portfolio with Portland’s Press Hotel
stories.excerpt: EOS Investors LLC, the real estate investment firm founded in 2017 by Jonathan Wang, has acquired The Press Hotel, Autograph Collection, a 110-key lifestyle hotel located in Portland, ME, for an undisclosed sum. The acquisition adds a top Portland hotel to EOS’s New England portfolio, encompassing 16 hotels, including eleven in Maine specifically. Simon Mais, CEO of EOS Hospitality, said, “The Press Hotel is a unique property that reflects Portland’s character. We are committed to building upon the hotel’s existing strengths and continuing to deliver an exceptional guest experience. Through EOS Hospitality’s operational platform, we see opportunities to refine the property’s offerings while preserving the qualities that have made it a well-known destination for visitors to Portland.” Originally constructed in 1947 as the headquarters of the Portland Press Herald, The Press Hotel was thoughtfully redeveloped into a luxury boutique hotel and today operates as part of Marriott’s Autograph Collection. It’s located within Portland’s Old Port neighborhood, providing access to the city’s restaurants, shops, waterfront attractions and cultural institutions. The post EOS Adds to New England Portfolio with Portland’s Press Hotel appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: EOS Investors acquires The Press Hotel (110 keys, Autograph Collection) in Portland, ME.
EOS now holds 16 New England hotels; 11 in Maine; Press Hotel is heritage property (1947 newspaper HQ).
Hotel acquisition, not multifamily; EOS focused on lifestyle hospitality.
stories.summary: EOS Investors, founded by Jonathan Wang, acquired The Press Hotel, a 110-key lifestyle hotel in Portland's Old Port, expanding its New England portfolio to 16 properties (11 in Maine). The hotel, originally the Portland Press Herald headquarters (1947), was redeveloped and operates under Marriott's Autograph Collection. EOS Hospitality CEO Simon Mais emphasized operational refinement while preserving character.
stories.impact: Hospitality acquisition, not multifamily development. No direct relevance to apartment supply, demand, or capital availability. Tangentially, increased hotel investment in Portland Old Port may signal broader market confidence in tourism and urban vitality, which can support adjacent mixed-use and residential demand, but is not a material development signal.

stories.title: Universal Music plans first US branded condo project at contentious Hill Country site
stories.excerpt: Branded condo development is taking off in Texas, and Universal Music Group is tossing its hat in the ring.  The “big three” record label is partnering with Austin developer White Rocks Entertainment to build UMusic Hotel & Private Residences Austin at a Hill Country site near Bee Cave, the Austin Business Journal reported.  The project, at 10549 SH 71, will feature 600 condo units, 150 hotel rooms and a 10,000-seat outdoor venue. Proposed amenities include professional recording studios, a nightclub, comedy club, indoor performance venue and two restaurants. It would be the first stateside hospitality project from Universal, which has […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Universal Music Group plans UMusic Hotel & Private Residences Austin: 600 condos, 150 hotel keys, 10K-seat venue.
Hill Country site (10549 SH 71, Bee Cave); $2.3M+ condo pricing; construction 2027–2030.
White Rocks Entertainment developer; water-reuse compliant after prior Violet Crown project failed over environmental pushback.
stories.summary: Universal Music Group partnered with Austin developer White Rocks Entertainment to develop UMusic Hotel & Private Residences Austin, a 600-unit condo + 150-room hotel + entertainment venue complex on a 71-acre Hill Country site near Bee Cave. Condo pricing starts at $2.3M. Construction targeted 2027–2030 pending approvals. Project addresses prior environmental/water-use objections by meeting 100% water reuse and 70% open space standards.
stories.impact: Branded luxury condo project (for-sale), not rental multifamily. However, Universal Music's entry into US residential development signals celebrity/brand IP crossing into real estate; if successful, may accelerate similar branded condo plays in high-demand second-tier metros (Austin), lifting land values and competitive pressure on nearby rental and mixed-use sites. The 2027 start date also indicates strong developer confidence in Austin's continued population influx and high-net-worth buyer demand despite macro uncertainty.

stories.title: Real estate getting drama treatment at Hulu
stories.excerpt: Real estate’s dramatics know no bounds, which will be on full display with a forthcoming Hulu series. George Stephanopoulos and Ali Wentworth are executive producing “The Pinnacle” at the streaming series, Deadline reported. The drama is being written by Damon Cardasis, who has been nominated for multiple awards.  The series — which licenses content from The Real Deal in its pilot — is set in New York City and follows the journey of a man who becomes exposed to the upper class while serving as a sobriety companion to the son of a wealthy family that lives on Billionaires’ Row. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Hulu drama 'The Pinnacle' greenlit; NYC-set, follows sobriety companion to wealthy family.
George Stephanopoulos executive produces; real estate subplot (Billionaires' Row setting).
Unrelated competing real estate drama series cancelled.
stories.summary: Hulu greenlighted 'The Pinnacle,' a drama series set in NYC centered on real estate and wealth, executive produced by George Stephanopoulos. The show draws from The Real Deal content and focuses on a sobriety companion embedded with a wealthy family on Billionaires' Row. A rival real estate drama (Edgar Ramirez, Jacob Scipio) was cancelled.
stories.impact: No impact on multifamily development. Entertainment industry coverage; mentions real estate as setting/narrative backdrop but does not affect supply, demand, financing, or construction costs.

stories.title: Altmark Group Sells Mott Haven Lots Destined for 99-Unit Redevelopment for $22M
stories.excerpt: Three adjacent lots in the Bronx’s Mott Haven neighborhood have been snapped up for a series of ground-up, 99-unit housing developments. The acquisition and accompanying plans join the city’s ever-growing ranks of 99-unit apartment deals, catalyzed by the construction wage requirements of the city’s controversial 485-x incentive. The transaction, filed Thursday, saw three parcels along East 132nd Street and Bruckner Boulevard trade for $21.7 million to LLC 122 Blvd 134 St. The buyer entity is tied to Anshel Fridman, head of the local real estate investment and development firm Nalcorp and general contractor Artist Construction. The seller was Bronx-based development, investment and property management firm Altmark Group, represented by Adi Altmark, a principal at the group, through the entity 122 Bruckner Development. The three lots in question are at 517 East 132nd Street, 519 East 132nd Street and 122 Bruckner Boulevard. The lots currently contain a mix of buildings zoned for industrial and specialty uses. Plans filed for the lots filed by Fridman with the New York City Department of Buildings call for three 14-story residential buildings, PincusCo reported. Each 99-unit building will span between 78,000 and 96,000 square feet, according to the filings. Nalcorp’s $21.7 million acquisition included a $15.72 million mortgage agreement with Israel-based bank Bank Hapoalim, according to property records. It is not clear if either party in the property transaction worked with an outside brokerage. The firms did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Altmark reports a portfolio of over 3 million square feet of residential and commercial real estate across the tri-state region. The group recently secured a $96 million refinancing for a completed Bronx apartment complex, the Motto, at 2455-2457 Third Avenue. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Altmark Group sells three Bronx lots (Mott Haven) to Nalcorp/Artist Construction for $21.7M.
Buyer: Anshel Fridman entity; plans two 99-unit buildings to avoid 485-x prevailing wage.
Sites: 517 E 132nd St, 519 E 132nd St, 122 Bruckner Blvd; mixed industrial/specialty zoning.
stories.summary: Three adjacent parcels in the Bronx's Mott Haven neighborhood traded for $21.7M to an LLC tied to Anshel Fridman (Nalcorp/Artist Construction). Plans include two 99-unit residential buildings, leveraging the prevailing-wage exemption under New York State's 485-x incentive. Seller was Altmark Group. Sites currently zoned industrial/specialty.
stories.impact: Consistent with trend flagged in [3]: developers fragmenting into dual 99-unit structures to escape 485-x wage burden. This deal further compresses available larger multifamily parcels in the Bronx and signals developer hesitation around full-scale projects under current wage policy. Watch for supply constipation if 99-unit workarounds proliferate; inability to achieve density/scale may reduce per-unit economics and delay starts, ultimately limiting rental supply in the Bronx despite apparent land activity.

stories.title: Dwight Mortgage Trust Lends $26M on Florida BTR Community
stories.excerpt: ARK Homes for Rent has sealed $26 million of bridge debt for a newly developed build-to-rent (BTR) community along Florida’s Gulf Coast, Commercial Observer has learned. Dwight Mortgage Trust provided the loan for Somerset Cove, a 143-unit townhome development in Spring Hill, Fla., 44 miles north of Downtown Tampa. The debt was originated by Dwight’s Katie Goldenberg and will fund an interest reserve to support the property through lease-up, according to the private lender. “We’re very excited about the Tampa market and have seen strong demand early on at Somerset Cove,” John Isakson, CEO of ARK Homes, said in a statement. “That response reflects both the quality of the homes and amenities we’re delivering, as well as the growing desire for purpose-built rental communities that offer the space, privacy, and lifestyle of a single-family home.” Located at 3933 Antigua Way, Somerset Cove comprises a mix of three-, four- and five-bedroom townhomes. Community amenities include a pool, a fitness center, a clubhouse, pickleball courts, a dog park and barbeque grilling stations. The Somerset Cove project is part of Miami-based Ark’s strategy to own build-to-rent communities throughout the Southeast. Ark, which was founded by Jordan Kavana, rebranded from its previous name, Transcendent Electra, in early 2023 and set out a goal then of acquiring $3 billion of new BTR communities over five years, as CO first reported at the time. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com
bullets: Dwight Mortgage Trust provides $26M bridge for ARK Homes' Somerset Cove (143 BTR townhomes).
Location: Spring Hill, FL, 44 mi north of Tampa; three-, four-, five-bedroom mix.
Loan funds interest reserve through lease-up; ARK reports strong early demand.
stories.summary: ARK Homes for Rent secured $26M in bridge financing from Dwight Mortgage Trust for Somerset Cove, a 143-unit build-to-rent townhome community in Spring Hill, FL (44 miles north of Tampa). The loan, originated by Katie Goldenberg, funds an interest reserve during lease-up. ARK CEO John Isakson cited strong early demand and the appeal of purpose-built single-family rental homes.
stories.impact: Positive signal for BTR capital availability and Tampa Bay area rental demand. Bridge financing at favorable terms for a newly delivered BTR community reflects lender confidence in Gulf Coast BTR fundamentals. Spring Hill's position north of Tampa signals suburban/exurban BTR expansion as Tampa metro rent growth drives demand outward. Monitor Northern Tampa Bay (Pasco County) for accelerating BTR starts and land sales; if Somerset Cove achieves strong lease-up, competitive BTR projects will follow, potentially lifting land costs and construction activity in the 30-50 mi ring around downtown Tampa.

stories.title: Trademark lassos Whole Foods for Southlake mixed-use development
stories.excerpt: Trademark Property Company officially secured Whole Foods Market to anchor its Southlake mixed-use project.  Terry Montesi’s Fort Worth-based development firm announced that the Austin-based organic grocer is coming to Shivers Farm, the 40-acre mixed-use development it’s advancing on White Chapel Boulevard, according to a release from Trademark. The news comes two days after Southlake City Council approved the project’s master development agreement.  Locals speculated that Whole Foods would anchor the project after a city council member referred to the future grocery store as Whole Foods during an October meeting, the Fort Worth Star-Telegram reported. In the release, the firm said […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Trademark Property secures Whole Foods anchor for Shivers Farm mixed-use (Southlake, TX).
40-acre project: 111K SF retail, 38K SF office, 37 single-family lots, 1-acre park.
Construction start summer 2026; completion end of 2027; first major grocer north of SH 114.
stories.summary: Fort Worth developer Trademark Property officially locked Whole Foods as anchor tenant for Shivers Farm, a 40-acre mixed-use development in Southlake, TX on White Chapel Boulevard. Project includes 111K SF retail, 38K SF office, 37 single-family lots, and a 3-acre pad for hotel/retail. Construction starts summer 2026; delivery end 2027. Whole Foods is the first major grocer to open north of State Highway 114 in Southlake.
stories.impact: Mixed-use/retail, not core multifamily. However, Whole Foods anchor in a previously underserved North Southlake submarket signals strong demographic/spending pull; the project's retail and office anchoring will support residential demand in the immediate vicinity. Developers should monitor Southlake north-of-SH-114 for follow-on multifamily opportunities, as Whole Foods typically de-risks surrounding residential positioning. Dallas area continues to lead U.S. in retail construction (7M SF pipeline); multifamily will likely follow major retail anchoring.

stories.title: Oakley Home Builders snags Burr Ridge equestrian estate for well below $10M ask
stories.excerpt: A Downers Grove-based developer acquired a Burr Ridge horse property for $6 million, taking a 40 percent discount from the original asking price to plot a new active-adult subdivision. Jacqueline Laase Parrillo sold Saddle Hill Farm, a 7.6-acre estate on County Line Road, to Oakley Home Builders. The property hit the market in August asking $10 million. Megan McCleary of Jameson Sotheby’s International Realty represented the seller. She declined to comment to Crain’s, which first reported the closing price. No broker was listed for the buyer. The deal extends Burr Ridge’s run of increased developer appetite for the few available […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Oakley Home Builders acquires Saddle Hill Farm (7.6 acres, Burr Ridge) for $6M (40% below ask).
Plans: 16-unit active-adult community (55+); 3,700 SF homes with accessible design, HOA exterior maintenance.
Original ask $10M; also adjacent Madison St site in Tower RE development (8 homes, $2.1–$2.7M each).
stories.summary: Oakley Home Builders purchased Saddle Hill Farm, a 7.6-acre Burr Ridge equestrian estate, for $6M—a 40% discount from the $10M asking price. The developer plans a 16-unit active-adult community (55+) with 3,700 SF homes, accessible features, and full HOA management. Nearby, Tower Real Estate Development is preparing an adjacent 4.7-acre lot for eight single-family homes ($2.1–$2.7M). Burr Ridge is seeing increased developer appetite for multi-acre estate sites.
stories.impact: Single-family for-sale housing, not multifamily rental development. However, the aggressive discount and market absorption suggest cooling demand for premium estate properties in affluent Chicagoland suburbs; Oakley's pivot to active-adult (55+) signals developer recognition of demographic shift away from large family homes toward smaller, service-rich communities. This trend may reduce pressure on outlying land values and free up development capital for urban-core rental infill in Chicago proper.

stories.title: Toyota’s $2B San Antonio expansion hangs on incentive vote
stories.excerpt: San Antonio leaders are confident that the city can seal the deal to secure Toyota’s next major investment in Texas. The San Antonio City Council is preparing to consider an economic incentives package tied to a proposed $2 billion Toyota manufacturing expansion, a project that would roughly double the size of the automaker’s South Side campus and create about 2,000 jobs. City officials increasingly believe they are in position to secure the investment, which has been circulating under the code name “Project Orca,” the San Antonio Business Journal reported.  Mayor Gina Ortiz Jones told the outlet that she is optimistic […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Toyota planning $2B manufacturing expansion in San Antonio; 2,000 new jobs; 'Project Orca.'
Would roughly double South Side campus size; City Council to vote on incentive package.
Automaker also investing $18.2M in new 32K SF office; long-term supplier ecosystem anchor.
stories.summary: San Antonio is negotiating a $2B Toyota manufacturing expansion (codename 'Project Orca') to roughly double the automaker's South Side campus and create ~2,000 jobs. City Council is preparing to vote on an economic incentive package. Toyota is simultaneously investing $18.2M in a new 32K SF office facility. The project would cement Toyota's decades-long commitment and expand the region's industrial/supplier network.
stories.impact: Major employment influx (2,000+ jobs) and capital investment in San Antonio will significantly lift regional population in-migration, household formation, and rental demand. Developers should immediately initiate acquisition/entitlement of multifamily sites along commute corridors to the South Side campus (esp. south and southwest suburbs) and near transit to downtown/employment centers. The project likely closes in 2H 2026; assume 2027–2028 construction phase with 2028–2030 employment ramp-up, creating a 2–3 year window to acquire, entitle, and pre-lease before rents accelerate. Focus on workforce housing (workforce/middle-income) in South San Antonio, east side, and northwest Austin suburbs.

stories.title: Bachow, partners greenlit for more resi units in downtown Fort Lauderdale
stories.excerpt: Noah Bachow and partners won city approval to build 20 percent more multifamily units at 11 Andrews Avenue, a 27-story mixed-use development in downtown Fort Lauderdale. The Fort Lauderdale City Commission unanimously voted Tuesday to approve the developers’ proposals to raise the number of units from 316 to 378, and to approve the development’s design in Fort Lauderdale’s high-density downtown zoning district.  The commission approved the density increase by allocating 62 “flex units” from a city-wide inventory of about 1,300. They allow the city, on a case-by-case basis, to make developments denser than standard limits imposed by zoning. Two entities, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Noah Bachow, partners win Fort Lauderdale city approval to add 62 units to 11 N Andrews Ave.
Increase: 316 to 378 units (20% density bump); 27-story mixed-use; Opportunity Zone; Brightline-adjacent.
Developers: Bachow Fam OZ Fund LLC + IRE Andrews Ave LLC; site acquisition 2023 for $8M.
stories.summary: Fort Lauderdale City Commission unanimously approved developers Noah Bachow and partners to increase density at 11 North Andrews Avenue from 316 to 378 units (+62 flex units), raising the tower to 27 stories in the high-density downtown district. The mixed-use project sits one block east of Brightline station and qualifies as an Opportunity Zone, enabling tax-advantaged capital raise. Developers acquired the former Goodyear Auto Service site for $8M in 2023.
stories.impact: Strong regulatory tailwind for dense, transit-oriented multifamily in downtown Fort Lauderdale. Approval of 62 flex units and 20% density increase signals city's receptivity to infill and Brightline-linked residential growth. Multifamily developers should accelerate due diligence on other Brightline-adjacent downtown parcels in Fort Lauderdale and Miami proper; the city's approval pattern and Opportunity Zone dynamics will attract both institutional and high-net-worth equity, tightening land competition and pushing acquisition prices up in the next 6–12 months. Monitor Brightline's ridership ramp and real estate activity in downtown West Palm, Miami, Fort Lauderdale to time starts optimally.
